coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent “off-on” sensor for acrylamide detection in toast based on red-emitting copper nanoclusters stabilized by bovine serum albumin
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Baoxin
Xia, Xiuhua
Han, Zhiqiang
Yu, Hang
Xie, Yufei
Guo, Yahui
Yao, Weirong
Qian, He
Cheng, Yuliang
coredata.dc:description: Acrylamide, as a Class 2A carcinogen, poses serious threats to human health. To achieve rapid and accurate determination of acrylamide in food, a ratiometric fluorescent “off–on” sensor was designed by incorporating red-emitting copper nanoclusters and glutathione. Copper nanoclusters with bimodal emission at 395 nm and 650 nm (excited at 310 nm) were synthesized by using bovine serum albumin as the ligand and ascorbic acid as the reductant. With glutathione addition, the fluorescence intensity at 650 nm gradually decreased, while the case at 395 nm slightly increased. The quenched fluorescence at 650 nm was subsequently restored by acrylamide through thiol-ene Michael addition reaction between acrylamide and glutathione. The constructed sensor showed excellent performance towards acrylamide detection in the range of 5–300 μM with a detection limit of 1.48 μM, and was further applied to real-sample detection of acrylamide in toast and exhibited good recoveries (90.29–101.30 %), indicating potential applications of this sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper nanoclusters
Bovine serum albumin
Red-emitting
Ratiometric fluorescence “turn-on” sensor
Acrylamide

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work reported in this article was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Project No. 2019YFC1606000), and Collaborative innovation center of food safety and quality control in Jiangsu Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Absorption spectra of BSA and CuNCs. (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CuNCs. The inset exhibited CuNCs under sunlight and 310 nm UV light.
(A) TEM image and nanoparticle diameter distribution, (B) FTIR spectrum, (C) XPS survey spectrum, (D) Cu2p spectrum of CuNCs.
Fluorescence spectra of CuNCs, CuNCs + AA, CuNCs + TCEP, CuNCs + AA + TCEP, CuNCs + GSH, CuNCs + GSH + TCEP, CuNCs + AA + GSH + TCEP and CuNCs + AA + GSH without TCEP, respectively.

(A) Fluorescence response of CuNCs/GSH/AA system with various AA concentrations (0–300 μM). (B) The relationship between F650/F395 and AA concentration in the range of 5–300 µM. (C) CuNCs fluorescence response in the presence of AA or interferents. (D) CuNCs fluorescence response in the presence of AA and interferents.
(A) Fluorescence lifetimes of CuNCs and CuNCs + GSH. (B) Absorption spectra of GSH, CuNCs and CuNCs + GSH. (C) The Stern-Volmer curves at 298 K, 308 K and 318 K.
Comparison of different analytical methods for AA sensing.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent “off-on” sensor for acrylamide detection in toast based on red-emitting copper nanoclusters stabilized by bovine serum albumin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control in Jiangsu Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center of Food Safety and Quality Control in Jiangsu Province, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wuxi Vocational Institute of Commerce, Wuxi 214122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Wuxi Vocational Institute of Commerce, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acrylamide, as a Class 2A carcinogen, poses serious threats to human health. To achieve rapid and accurate determination of acrylamide in food, a ratiometric fluorescent “off–on” sensor was designed by incorporating red-emitting copper nanoclusters and glutathione. Copper nanoclusters with bimodal emission at 395 nm and 650 nm (excited at 310 nm) were synthesized by using bovine serum albumin as the ligand and ascorbic acid as the reductant. With glutathione addition, the fluorescence intensity at 650 nm gradually decreased, while the case at 395 nm slightly increased. The quenched fluorescence at 650 nm was subsequently restored by acrylamide through thiol-ene Michael addition reaction between acrylamide and glutathione. The constructed sensor showed excellent performance towards acrylamide detection in the range of 5–300 μM with a detection limit of 1.48 μM, and was further applied to real-sample detection of acrylamide in toast and exhibited good recoveries (90.29–101.30 %), indicating potential applications of this sensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Acrylamide (AA) is a water-soluble small molecule with potential neurotoxicity, reproductive toxicity, genotoxicity and carcinogenicity, and is documented as a Class 2A carcinogen by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC). (Khorshidian, Yousefi, Shadnoush, Siadat, Mohammadi, & Mortazavian, 2020; Koszucka, Nowak, Nowak, & Motyl, 2019; Pundir, Yadav, & Chhillar, 2019). AA in food mainly originates from the side reaction of Maillard reaction (Y. Zhang & Zhang, 2007) and is commonly found in food that has been processed thermally after frying and baking, such as French fries (Burhan & Hulya, 2021), coffee (Kocadağlı & Gökmen, 2022), and bread (Mollakhalili-Meybodi, Khorshidian, Nematollahi, & Arab, 2021). In 2017, the European Union (EU) updated the maximum permissible concentration for AA in different foods with potato crisps of 750 μg/kg, roast coffee of 400 μg/kg, and soft bread of 50 μg/kg (Rayappa, Viswanathan, Rattu, & Krishna, 2021). However, thermally processed foods will inevitably contain some amount of AA. Therefore, it is crucial for AA to be accurately and quickly detected in food.

The existing methods for quantitative analysis of AA in food include liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Oellig, Gottstein, & Granvogl, 2022), gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (Norouzi, Kamankesh, Mohammadi, & Attaran, 2018), Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Cheng, Zhang, Wang, Wang, Su, & Xie, 2018), electrochemical methods (Lau, Ng, Yusof, Liu, Alias, & Khor, 2019), etc. While these methods for AA analysis have shown satisfactory results, they have certain drawbacks such as expensive instruments, complicated operations, long detection time, and low reproducibility. However, in recent years, rapid and convenient detection methods for AA determination have been developed quickly (Jia, Li, Han, Chen, Yao, Ning, Gao, & Fan, 2021). The fluorescent analysis has gained popularity due to its high sensitivity, selectivity, simple operation and rapid detection, etc. Despite the above-mentioned strengths, most fluorescence analysis methods for AA detection report a single signal response with fluorescence enhancement or quenching (Asnaashari, Esmaeilzadeh Kenari, Farahmandfar, Taghdisi, & Abnous, 2018; Wei, Liu, Pu, & Sun, 2020; Wei, Zhang, Liu, Pu, & Sun, 2021), which is susceptible to environmental interference. The ratiometric fluorescence is a strategy of measuring the ratio of emission intensity of two different wavelengths at the same time. The proportional fluorescence sensor which has been widely applied in the detection of various substances can effectively avoid spontaneous fluorescence and background interference, and improve the accuracy of the detection system (Mojtaba, Foroozan, Fatemeh, Hosna, Farimah, Mosslim, Peter, & Hosein, 2023; S. Wang, Guo, Chen, Wang, & Song, 2022). It provides a reliable, fast and accurate method for AA determination in food.

Copper nanoclusters (CuNCs), consisting of a few to several hundred copper atoms, are characterized by high fluorescence stability, good biocompatibility and large Stokes shift, and have obvious economic advantages over noble metal nanoclusters, which have been widely involved in the assessment of various substances (Zhao & Li, 2020). Wang et al. (H. B. Wang, Tao, Wu, Zhang, & Liu, 2022) synthesized blue-emitting CuNCs using glutathione as the ligand and ascorbic acid as the reductant to achieve the detection of p-nitrophenol. Cai et al. (Cai, Li, Wang, Min, Wen, Fu, et al., 2022) synthesized green-emitting polyethyleneimine-templated CuNCs using hydrazine hydrate as the reducing agent and constructed a fluorescent sensor for hygromycin determination. At present, many existing CuNCs are blue-emitting or green- emitting. For improving the anti-interference ability of detection, red-emitting CuNCs become a great choice. Although some red-emitting CuNCs have been reported, they have a few shortcomings, such as toxic reagents involved in the synthesis process and poor fluorescence stability (Wang, Yang, Mi, Zhang, Dou, Liu, Hu, Xue et al., 2021). There is an urgent need for a green synthesis and stable fluorescence of red-emitting CuNCs. If CuNCs have bimodal emission, it will provide great convenience for the construction of proportional fluorescence sensing systems. So far, there have been few reports on potential applications of CuNCs for AA monitoring, much less any construction of proportional fluorescence sensors based on CuNCs to provide feasible methods for the detection of AA in food.

In this study, CuNCs with bimodal emission were synthesized using bovine serum albumin (BSA) as the ligand and ascorbic acid (ASA) as the reducing agent and showed an optimal excitation at the wavelength of 310 nm (the maximum fluorescence emission obtained at 650 nm) and bimodal emission at the wavelength of both 395 nm and 650 nm. Interestingly, glutathione (GSH) could quench the emission at 650 nm, while enhancing the fluorescence intensity at 395 nm. With the addition of AA, the fluorescence emission could be restored due to thiol-ene Michael addition reaction between AA and GSH. Utilizing the above principle, a ratiometric fluorescent “off–on” sensor was developed for the rapid and precise detection of AA in food. Aiming at the deficiency of existing CuNCs and the single signal response of existing AA detection systems, we synthesized the red-emitting CuNCs with excellent fluorescence performance and double-peak emission, which showed a good performance as a proportional fluorescence probe to detect AA in toast.

Bovine serum albumin (BSA), methacrylamide (MA), propionic acid (PA), dl-alanine (dl-Ala) and l-asparagine (l-Asn) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Copper sulfate (CuSO4), ascorbic acid (ASA), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acrylamide (AA), tris(2-carboxyethyl) phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP), glycine (Gly), sucrose (Suc), glucose (Glu), fructose (Fru), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4·2H2O), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4·2H2O), n-hexane (C6H14), quinine sulfate (QS), sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and acetonitrile (C2H3N) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS), glutathione (GSH), acrylic acid (Aa) and potassium sorbate (PS) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). QuEChERS buffer salt (containing 4 g of MgSO4 and 1 g of NaCl) was purchased from High Education Research Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All the reagents were at last of analytical grade and used without further purification. The water used in all experiments was Watson's distilled water, purchased from Watson's Distilled Water Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

Synthesis of BSA-CuNCs: 10 mL of CuSO4 aqueous solution (5 mM) was added to 10 mL of BSA aqueous solution (30 mg/mL) drop by drop and stirred for 2 min at room temperature. The color of the mixed solution turned milky white. Subsequently, 500 μL of ASA aqueous solution (1 M) was added and the system pH was adjusted to 11 by NaOH aqueous solution (1 M). The mixed solution was placed in a water bath at 55 °C and stirred for 100 min sufficiently. The color of the mixed solution changed to brownish yellow eventually. The mixed solution was collected and dialyzed using a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 12 h. The BSA-CuNCs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

Synthesis of CMCS@BSA-CuNCs: 0.3 g of CMCS was added to 10 mL of BSA aqueous solution (30 mg/mL) and stirred for 10 min at room temperature. All other steps are identical to the synthesis of BSA-CuNCs. The CMCS@BSA-CuNCs were obtained which were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of CuNCs were acquired using a FEI Tecnai G2F 20 apparatus (FEI Ltd., U.S.A.). The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was obtained between 4000 and 400 cm−1 on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Ltd., U.S.A.). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were acquired on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS (Thermo Scientific Ltd., U.S.A.). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption was measured through UV-1800 spectrophotometers (Shimadzu Ltd., Japan). The fluorescence spectra were measured using RF-6000 spectrophotometers (Shimadzu Ltd., Japan).

The quantum yield of CuNCs was obtained with reference to QS (QY = 54 %). The quantum yield of CuNCs was calculated from the equation. (1)
(1)
QY
s
=
F s
×
A r
×
η s
×
QY
r
F r
×
A s
×
η r
where QY, F, A, and η denote the quantum yield, integrated area of the target fluorescence intensity, absorbance, and refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The subscripts s and r denote the sample and reference, respectively.

GSH quenching: 10 μL CMCS@BSA-CuNCs stock solution, 490 μL of PBS buffer (100 mM, PH = 7) and 500 μL of GSH with different concentrations (0–400 μM) was mixed. The mixture was shaken fully and incubated at room temperature for 5 min. Then fluorescence spectra of the solutions were scanned at 310 nm as the excitation wavelength and recorded the wavelength range of wavelength from 370 nm to 770 nm.

Detection of AA: referred to the method of Zhuang et al. with slight modification (Zhuang, Ma, Huang, Han, Wang, & Zhang, 2021). AA underwent thiol-ene Michael addition reaction with GSH as follows:320 μM of GSH (1 equivalent) and TCEP (0.07 equivalent) were mixed in PBS buffer (5 mM, PH = 9) with different concentrations of AA (0–300 μM) and the reaction lasted for 3 h at 25 °C. 10 μL of CMCS@BSA-CuNCs stock solution, and 410 μL of PBS buffer (100 mM, PH = 7) were added to 580 μL of thiol-ene Michael addition reaction solution. The mixture was shaken fully and incubated at room temperature for 5 min. Then fluorescence spectra of these solutions were scanned at 310 nm as the excitation wavelength and recorded the wavelength range of wavelength from 370 nm to 770 nm.

To observe the selectivity of the sensor, the AA (300 μM) was replaced with MA, Aa, PA, Ala, Gly, PS, Asn, Suc, Glu and Fru (600 μM) respectively and performed the same operation. Additionally, to test the anti-interference of the sensor, MA, Aa, PA, Ala, Gly, PS, Asn, Suc, Glu and Fru (600 μM) were added as interfering substances simultaneously with AA (300 μM) and perform the same operation.

A kind of toast was purchased from a local supermarket (Wuxi, China). The toast was pretreated by referring to Gan et al. with slight modification (Gan, Zhang, Arslan, Hu, Zhang, Li, Shi, & Zou, 2022). Specifically, 3 g of toast was collected and crushed before being added to 10 mL of hexane to remove lipids. The sample was then sonicated with an ultrasonic cleaner for 10 min. Following this, 10 mL of an extractant mixture consisting of water and acetonitrile (v:v = 1:1) was added and shaken for 10 min. The QuEChERS buffer salt was subsequently added and the mixture was sonicated to obtain a homogeneous solution, The solution was then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant (acetonitrile phase) was evaporated under nitrogen flow at 40 °C. The final volume of the solution was 6 mL achieved by adding distilled water and the solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane.

1000 μL of the above filtrate was mixed with 200 μL of the standard solutions of AA (PBS as solvent, pH = 9, 30 mM) to make the final spiked concentrations of AA to 0, 10, 50 and 100 μΜ, respectively.

320 μM of GSH (1 equivalent) and TCEP (0.07 equivalent) were mixed in the sample solutions with different spiked concentrations of AA (0, 10, 50, 100 μΜ) and reacted at 25 °C for 3 h. After the above reaction was completed, 10 μL of CMCS@BSA-CuNCs stock solution and 410 μL of PBS buffer (100 mM, PH = 7) were added to 580 μL of the above reaction solution. The mixture was shaken fully and incubated at room temperature for 5 min. Then fluorescence spectra of these solutions were scanned at 310 nm as the excitation wavelength and recorded the wavelength range of wavelength from 370 nm to 770 nm.

All measurements were conducted in triplicate and results were expressed as means ± standard deviation (SD). Origin 2023b software (OriginLab Co., MA, USA) was used for data processing and analysis. The particle size of CuNCs in TEM images was measured using Nano_Measurer 1.2.5 software (School of Food Science and Technology, Jiangnan University, China).

In this work, BSA-CuNCs with bimodal emission were synthesized in green by hydrothermal reduction using CuSO4, BSA, ASA and NaOH as raw materials. Water-soluble BSA was used as a stabilizer, and its functional groups such as -SH, -NH2 and -COOH complexed with Cu (Ⅱ). The use of ASA which is considered a green reductant in this work is quite different from other works using hydrazine hydrate or sodium borohydride as reducing agents (Aparna, Devi, Anjana, Nebu, & George, 2019; B. Wang, Gui, Jin, He, & Wang, 2017). The experimental conditions for BSA-CuNCs fabrication were optimized, including system pH, reaction temperature, the concentration of ASA, the concentration of BSA and reaction time. The pH affects the synthesis of BSA-CuNCs by changing the conformation of BSA. As shown in Fig.S1A, the optimal system pH was 11 and the fluorescence intensity at 650 nm decreased rapidly beyond the critical value, because the structure of BSA-CuNCs may be destroyed probably in a strongly alkaline environment (Dixon & Egusa, 2018). The reaction rate can be increased by raising the temperature within a certain range, but exceeding the critical temperature may lead to the decomposition of CuNCs. As shown in Fig.S1B, the fluorescence intensity reached its peak at 55 °C, which is a relatively low synthesis temperature compared to other CuNCs. (Wu, Zhang, & Li, 2018). The core of BSA-CuNCs is composed of Cu (0) and Cu (Ⅰ). Cu (Ⅱ) is reduced in the system with a suitable reduction capacity, which decides the concentration of ASA. As shown in Fig.S1C, 1 M was used as the optimal concentration of ASA. When the concentration of ASA was below 1 M, the reductive capacity was too weak to completely reduce Cu (Ⅱ). When the concentration of ASA exceeded 1 M, there are large-sized particles in strong reducing conditions. As the stabilizer, BSA captured as many Cu (Ⅱ) as possible. As shown in Fig.S1D, the fluorescence intensity increased with increasing concentration of BSA. When the concentration of BSA was 30 mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity reached the peak, but it decreased gradually over this value, which may be attributed to the huge spatial effect of excessive BSA (C. Wang, et al., 2021). Reaction time is also an important parameter for the synthesis of BSA-CuNCs. As shown in Fig.S1E, the fluorescence intensity continued to increase during the first 100 min. After reaching 100 min, the fluorescence intensity almost stopped increasing. Therefore, the optimal time for synthesis was 100 min, which was shorter than that of most CuNCs (Cai, et al., 2022; Hu, He, Han, Ge, Song, & Zhou, 2018; H. B. Wang, Tao, Wu, Zhang, & Liu, 2022). Interestingly, the addition of CMCS before the start of the system reaction greatly can enhance the fluorescence intensity of BSA-CuNCs and the enhancement of fluorescence intensity may be attributed to increasing of the rigidity of BSA (Chen, Li, Huang, & Jia, 2021). As shown in Fig.S1F, the fluorescence intensity reached the maximum when CMCS reached 0.3 g. More importantly, the storage stability of BSA-CuNCs was extended from 2 days to 14 days (Fig.S2A). Therefore, the optimal conditions for the synthesis of CMCS@BSA-CuNCs were as follows: 0.3 g of CMCS, 10 mL of CuSO4 aqueous solution (5 mM), 10 mL of BSA aqueous solution (30 mg/mL), 500 μL of ASA aqueous solution (1 M), the system pH was 11, and the reaction time was 100 min.

The following discussion was characterized as CMCS@BSA-CuNCs (abbreviated as CuNCs in the subsequent paragraphs) and the results of the characterization of BSA-CuNCs would not be described. CuNCs were prepared under optimal conditions and their absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were measured in order to demonstrate the synthesis of CuNCs. As shown in Fig. 1 A, BSA had a characteristic absorption band located at 275 nm and CuNCs had a characteristic absorption band located at 260 nm. We found that the absorption at 300–450 nm is significantly stronger than that of BSA. Fig. 1B showed that the optimal excitation wavelength of CuNCs was at 310 nm and the optimal emission wavelengths were at 395 nm and 650 nm, respectively. The CuNCs solution was brownish under sunlight illumination and showed bright red fluorescence upon excitation at 310 nm (inset of Fig. 1B). The emission peak of 395 nm was attributed to BSA (C. Wang, et al., 2021). The emission peak of 650 nm was attributed to the charge transfer between the copper core and the ligand and the triplet emission of CuNCs (Mu, Peng, Shi, Zhang, & Jia, 2021). The quantum yield of the fluorescence emission at 650 nm for CuNCs is 1.38 % using QS (QY = 54 %) as a reference.

The microstructure and chemical composition of CuNCs were characterized by TEM, FTIR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the TEM images of CuNCs were spherical in shape and exhibited that the particle sizes were mainly distributed between 1.6 and 2.4 nm with an average particle size of 2.16 nm based on analysis of 220 particles (Fig. 2A, left inset). The HRTEM images of CuNCs (Fig. 2A, right inset) showed a lattice fringe with the lattice spacing of 0.200 nm, corresponding to the (111) crystal plane of Cu (0) (Shao, Xiong, Cao, Zhang, Luo, & Liu, 2021). Fig. 2B showed that the FTIR spectra of CuNCs were almost identical to those of BSA, indicating that they both had the same functional groups, and the characteristic peaks at 3445, 1647, and 2960 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C-OH, CO, and NH bonds, respectively (B. Wang, Gui, Jin, He, & Wang, 2017). XPS spectra were used to analyze the elemental composition and chemical states in CuNCs, and Fig. 2C indicated that the CuNCs contained S, C, N, O, and Cu. The five peaks at 163.79, 285.35, 399.80, 531.46 and 932.26 eV were assigned to the states of S2p, C1s, N1s, O1s, and Cu2p, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2D Cu2p spectrum missed the characteristic peak of 942 eV, indicating that there was no Cu (Ⅱ) in CuNCs. The characteristic peaks at 932.26 eV and 952.48 eV, corresponded to the Cu2p3/2 and Cu2p1/2, demonstrating the existence of Cu (0) and Cu (Ⅰ) in CuNCs (Aparna, Devi, Anjana, Nebu, & George, 2019).

The stability of CuNCs in the environment determined their potential applications. Therefore, the stability of CuNCs under different conditions, including storage stability, acid and alkali resistance, salt resistance and photobleaching resistance, was explored. As shown in Fig. S2A, when stored at 4 °C, the fluorescence intensity at 650 nm of CuNCs did not change significantly within 14 days. Fig. S2B showed CuNCs remained stable at the pH ranged from 6 to 9. As shown in Fig. S2C & D, the fluorescence intensity of CuNCs no significant difference with the concentration of NaCl reached 3 M and UV irradiation (360 nm) for up to 120 min, confirming excellent salt resistance and photobleaching resistance.

The sensor in this study was based on thiol-ene Michael addition reaction between AA and GSH (Rong, Ali, Ouyang, Wang, Wang, & Chen, 2021; Tong, Cornwell, & Means, 2004). GSH quenched the fluorescence emission at 650 nm of CuNCs and enhance the fluorescence emission at 395 nm. When AA was present, AA formed an adduct with GSH, and the fluorescence emission at 650 nm and 395 nm was restored. To verify the feasibility of the detection strategy, the sensing effects of different systems were explored. As shown in Fig. 3 , AA, TCEP and the mixture of both had basically no effect on the fluorescence emission at 650 nm and 395 nm. The fluorescence emission at 650 nm was significantly quenched while the case at 395 nm was slightly enhanced when GSH was added. With GSH and TCEP simultaneously added, the change of fluorescence emission was almost the same as that of GSH added alone. When GSH and AA were added at the same time, the fluorescence emission was recovered, but the extent was negligible. Only when GSH, AA and TCEP were simultaneously added, the fluorescence emission could be recovered significantly, which verified the feasibility of the detection strategy.

To obtain the best performance, we optimized the system pH value, concentration of GSH and reaction time based on the change of fluorescence intensity at 650 nm and 395 nm caused by GSH. The fluorescence intensity was distinct at different pH values. As shown in Fig. S3A, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F650/F395) was the lowest at pH value of 7, so 7 was chosen as the optimal pH value in subsequent experiments. The effect of GSH concentration on fluorescence intensity was investigated. As shown in Fig. S3B, the F650/F395 gradually decreased with the increase of GSH concentration, and the F650/F395 no longer changed when GSH concentration reached 320 μM, so 320 μM was selected as the best GSH concentration for subsequent experiments. Fig. S3C showed the F650/F395 rapidly decreased within the first 5 min after the addition of GSH. The F650/F395 kept stable after that and remained constant for 25 min, so 5 min was selected as the optimal reaction time. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S4, we found that F650/F395 showed a strong linear relationship with GSH concentration in the range of both 1–20 μM and 20–320 μM, which demonstrated the potential for GSH detection.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, different concentrations of AA were firstly reacted with GSH (320 μM) at 25 °C for 3 h, followed by the addition of PBS buffer and CuNCs. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with the increase of AA concentration from 0 to 300 μM, the fluorescence emission at 395 nm attenuated, while the fluorescence emission at 650 nm enhanced. The F650/F395 gradually increased, which was attributed to the formation of a chemical bond between AA and the sulfhydryl group of GSH. The F650/F395 showed a strong linear relationship with AA concentration in the range of 5–300 μM (Fig. 4B). The linear equation was F650/F395 = 0.00694C + 1.15464, R2 = 0.99843, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the following equation, LOD = 3 s/k, where s is the standard deviation of the blank solution and k is the slope of linear equation. The LOD of the sensor was 1.48 μM, which met the requirements of AA detection in food.

In the face of the complex detection environment, high selectivity and strong anti-interference capability were important indicators for practical applications of the sensing system. Therefore, MA, Aa, PA, dl-Ala, Gly, PS, l-Asn, Suc, Glu and Fru were used to evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the assay system. As shown in Fig. 4C, when the above different substances were added separately, even though the concentration of the added substances (600 μM) was significantly higher than that of AA (300 μM), F650/F395 was significantly improved only when AA was added. As shown in Fig. 4D, when the above substances were added as interfering substances (600 μM), the detection system still showed good detection results. The above results showed that the sensor had good selectivity and anti-interference ability.

Table 1 summarizes the various detection methods for AA in food. Conventional chromatographic methods have the disadvantages of requiring large instruments, complicated operation, and long detection time. Electrochemical methods and SERS have the characteristics of sensitive and rapid detection, but the synthesis of electrochemical materials is relatively complex and SERS shows low repeatability. Most of the fluorescence detection methods are single-signal responsive and have poor resistance to background interference. In comparison, this study provides a method for AA detection with simple material synthesis, rapid detection, high selectivity, and strong anti-interference capability.

To validate the application of the proportional fluorescence sensor in real samples, AA in toast was detected. The results of the spiked recovery experiment are presented in Table S1 (Supplementary Material). In particular, this recovery only was on behalf of the detection method, which could be modified according to the extraction method applied. The results with recovery of 90.29–101.30 % and relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.18–3.31 % indicated that the developed ratiometric fluorescent “off–on” sensor had excellent potential for the detection of AA in toast.

The mechanisms of fluorescence quenching include static quenching, dynamic quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) (Liu, Ding, Zhang, & Ding, 2019). First, the effect of GSH on the fluorescence emission at 650 nm was explored. The fluorescence lifetime was used as an important index to investigate the quenching mechanism. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence lifetimes of CuNCs without and with the addition of GSH were 3.10 μs and 2.83 μs, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime changed, which was a feature of dynamic quenching (Zu, Yan, Bai, Xu, Wang, Huang, & Zhou, 2017). The absorption spectra were further discussed as shown in Fig. 5B, and it was found that the absorption peaks did not change after the addition of GSH, confirming dynamic quenching (B. Zhang, Zou, Li, Lu, Wang, & Ma, 2022). Besides, as shown in Fig. 5C, the linear regression equation following Stern-Volmer relation was also plotted (F0/F = 1 + KSV[Q], where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of pesticide, respectively, KSV was dynamic quenching constant, [Q] was concentration of pesticide) and it was found KSV increased with the increase of temperature, which was consistent with the characteristics of dynamic quenching (Zhang, Liu, Wang, Ma, Zhou, Wang, Wang, & Zhang, 2018). To sum up, the quenching of CuNCs by GSH belongs to dynamic quenching. For the enhancement of fluorescence emission at 395 nm, which may be attributed to FRET. The fluorescence emission at 650 nm was quenched, resulting in a weaker FRET and enhanced fluorescence emission at 395 nm (Yao-Yao, Jun, Li, Jie, Jing, & Zhi-Qi, 2021). The addition of AA and thiol-ene Michael addition reaction led to the formation of adducts between AA and GSH, which ultimately restored the fluorescence emission at 650 nm.

In summary, we synthesized red-emitting CuNCs with stable fluorescence properties and bimodal emission, and proposed a proportional fluorescent method to detect AA in toast. In this study, red-emitting CuNCs with bimodal emission at 395 nm and 650 nm were synthesized by a green hydrothermal method. Based on the interaction between GSH and CuNCs as well as thiol-ene Michael-addition reaction between AA and GSH, a proportional fluorescent sensor for AA detection was constructed, which exhibited good linearity in the range from 5 to 300 μM of AA concentration with a LOD of 1.48 μM. The sensor was further used for the detection of AA in toast with good spiked recoveries (90.29–101.30 %) and relative standard deviations (2.18–3.31 %). Overall, the as-constructed sensor has great potential for quantitative determination of AA in toast.

Baoxin Cheng: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Xiuhua Xia: Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Zhiqiang Han: Resources, Formal analysis, Software. Hang Yu: Resources, Software. Yufei Xie: Validation. Yahui Guo: Resources. Weirong Yao: Writing – review & editing. He Qian: Supervision. Yuliang Cheng: Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work reported in this article was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (Project No. 2019YFC1606000), and Collaborative innovation center of food safety and quality control in Jiangsu Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137878.
